w: John Kaufman's 'The Diver: Regeneration' stands fifteen feet tall in the river itself — built from some 300 metres of galvanised steel banding and three thousand bolts, weighing three tons, and believed the only sculpture standing in the Thames. It honours his grandfather, a clearance diver in the London Docks around 1900, and is submerged at every high tide. Kaufman died in 2002, soon after finishing it.

s: not the D-Day barges everyone thinks
w: Around sixteen WWII ferro-concrete barges lie half-sunk in the Thames mud as 1953 flood defences. The on-site plaque's story — Mulberry harbour pieces towed back from Normandy — is a myth: these were 1940 petrol barges that never crossed to France. Wintering pipits roost on them now. Cross the deep silt only in sturdy boots.

Medieval grazing marsh used as a military firing range for nearly a century before the RSPB reopened it in 2006. Brick stop-butts still pocked with .303 bullet holes survive among the reeds, with wide skies and a clean view to Canary Wharf across the water. The visitor centre's future has been reported under review.

A boomerang-shaped concrete pier, around 360 metres of it, built in the 1950s as a cargo wharf, abandoned, then reopened to walkers in 1999 — London's longest pier on the Thames. Erith's brief 1840s career as a riverside resort was killed by the railway, then finished off by Crossness sewage flowing upstream from 1865.

s: marsh wilderness beside the Great Stink's cure
w: A 25-hectare reserve of grazing marsh and Bexley's largest reedbeds — water voles, the rare shrill carder bee, big estuary skies — sitting right beside the eastern terminus of Bazalgette's Southern Outfall Sewer, opened in 1865 to cure the Great Stink. Wilderness engineered out of a city's effluent.

q: Apollo Banana Leaf Tooting Sri Lankan
w: A no-frills, BYOB family Sri Lankan and South Indian curry house beloved of London's Tamil community — squid curry, mutton kothu, lurid-green Sri Lankan cream soda, mains for a few pounds, cash only. The name riffs on the NASA missions; the pilgrimage is real, with food bloggers and r/london regulars in the same queue.

A local-legend Pakistani BYOB grill running since the mid-1990s — the first venue at the south end of Tooting's curry corridor — turning out charcoal skewers and meat karahi. Publicly a favourite of Tooting's most famous son, the Mayor of London.

A canteen-bright Chennai-style café, widely rated for the best dosas in London, with Tamil films running on the flatscreen and idly going two-for-not-very-much. Time Out called it close to a perfect dosa; the regulars never doubted it.

q: Jaffna House Tooting Sri Lankan Tamil
w: A Jaffna Tamil institution on the curry corridor for cheap mutton rolls, fish cutlets, chicken kotthu and palm-sugar watalappam custard — the snacks and sweets a Sri Lankan diaspora actually grew up on, served without ceremony.

South London's original covered indoor market, founded in 1930 — a multicultural warren of Jamaican, Mauritian and Guyanese traders, with blue plaques over the entrances reading 'A community of all, for all. We are Tooting.' Lonely Planet once named the area among the coolest places on earth; the market is why.

q: O Cantinho de Portugal Stockwell Little Portugal
w: A family-run Portuguese restaurant in Lambeth's Little Portugal — London's largest, mostly Madeiran, Portuguese-speaking community — split Lisbon-style with the football on Portuguese TV. Bacalhau com natas, moelas, grilled sardines, and a room that feels imported wholesale from the Atlantic.

q: Madeira Patisserie South Lambeth Road pastel de nata
w: Opened in 1988, the wholesale powerhouse behind a startling share of the UK's pastéis de nata — blasted at 300 degrees until the custard scorches and the pastry shatters. Time Out has called these among London's best pastries; half the city has eaten one without knowing it came from a South Lambeth Road counter.

q: The Crane Wandsworth oldest pub haunted
w: Wandsworth's oldest pub, once called the most haunted building in the borough — reputedly walked by a boy in short trousers in the bar and a woman who passes straight through the attic wall. Reported closed of late; verify the trading status before you cross town for a pint with a ghost.

q: Tooting ghost signs Mitcham Road 20th Century Gowns
w: A pair of ghost signs revealed when a billboard came down on Mitcham Road: '20th Century Gowns' painted over 'Dallas Star Tested Band Instruments', a 1920s HMV dealership. Two vanished trades, one wall, surfacing together after decades in the dark.

q: Royal Victoria Patriotic Building Le Gothique Wandsworth
w: A vast 1859 Gothic pile, built as an asylum for the orphans of the Crimean War, later a WWII MI5 and MI6 interrogation centre where arrivals to Britain were screened for spies. It now houses the Le Gothique restaurant, complete with a resident ghost named Charlotte and turrets that don't quite belong to any century you can name.

q: Crossness Pumping Station Bazalgette beam engines
w: Bazalgette and Charles Driver's 1865 Romanesque temple of polychrome cast iron — home to the largest remaining rotative beam engines in the world, with 52-ton flywheels named Victoria, Prince Consort, Albert Edward and Alexandra. Grade I-listed, opened to the public in 2016, with volunteer steaming days and a narrow-gauge 'Excrement Express'. A cathedral built to move a city's filth.

One of London's finest surviving Jacobean mansions, built 1607–12 for Prince Henry's tutor Sir Adam Newton. The carved faces on the bannister get uglier as you climb — the evil spirits, it was held, worsened the higher you went. A 1607 mulberry from James I's doomed silk scheme survives, and a summerhouse that ended its days as a 1930s public toilet.

A bright-yellow Persian emporium-cum-café opened around 1995 by Sally Butcher and her Iranian husband Jamshid — once 'the only Iranian shop south of the river'. Cult sweets, BYOB vegetarian food, shelves of rosewater and dried limes, and Sally's best-selling cookbooks written from behind the till.

q: Pho Hanoi Deptford Vietnamese High Street
w: A modest, authentic Vietnamese spot on Deptford High Street, part of a community descended from the late-1970s refugees who settled around Evelyn Street. Pho, bún bò Huế, chả lá lốt — the cooking of a boat-people diaspora that quietly made this stretch of south-east London its own.

q: M Manze Deptford High Street pie and mash
w: A Grade II-listed eel, pie and mash shop opened in 1914 by Pantaleone Manze of the Anglo-Italian Manze dynasty — original terrazzo, marble tables and a black-glass shopfront, all unchanged. Distinct from the Manze shops at Tower Bridge: same Ravello-immigrant root, separate enterprise. Reported closing on its owner's retirement, but the listed interior endures.

The first ocakbaşı in London, opened in the late 1980s — a poky BYOB whose entrance grill smokes halfway down Arcola Street and whose chefs wear 'simply the best' t-shirts. The original that an entire Turkish grill scene grew out of; Mangal 2 around the corner is the offspring, not the source.

q: Nunhead District Municipal Museum Telegraph Hill Festival
w: A house transformed each Telegraph Hill Festival into a fake municipal museum — complete with an invented Victorian industrialist, George Gellatly, a lost post-punk band called The Nunhead Cemeteries, and fictional blue plaques scattered across the neighbourhood. A whole local history, entirely made up, and lovingly believed. Festival-only.

q: Spriggan Parkland Walk Crouch End sculpture
w: Marilyn Collins's 1993 Cornish spriggan crawls out of a railway arch on the disused Finsbury Park–Alexandra Palace line, inspired by a local legend of a ghostly 'goat-man'. Often linked to Stephen King's story 'Crouch End', though the tale predates the sculpture by a decade — so the spriggan can't have inspired King, only the walk might have. Either way, it watches you pass.

A Grade II-listed 1886 Wood Bros. beam engine in a former sewage pumping station in Markfield Park, where the buried River Moselle meets the Lea. On steam days the great beam still turns over, exactly as it did when it was lifting north London's waste — Victorian engineering kept alive by volunteers.

A Grade I sixteenth-century manor with an enigmatic Tudor round tower whose purpose is still argued over — dovecote, hawk mews, nobody is sure. Now Haringey's local museum, it holds the Rowland Hill collection charting the postal reform that gave the world the penny stamp. Tudor brick marooned in modern Tottenham.

q: Newington Green Unitarian Church Mary Wollstonecraft
w: London's oldest Nonconformist place of worship still in use, from 1708. Richard Price preached radical sermons here; Mary Wollstonecraft had her political awakening in the congregation. The terrace opposite, of 1658, is London's oldest surviving brick row, and Maggi Hambling's contested Wollstonecraft sculpture stands on the green outside.

q: Church Studios Crouch Hill Dave Stewart
w: A nineteenth-century church converted into a recording studio by Eurythmics' Dave Stewart in the 1980s, and since used by Adele, Radiohead, Bowie and Massive Attack. Local legend has Bob Dylan knocking on the wrong door and ending up taking tea with a startled Crouch End resident. A working studio — admire it from the street.

q: Gokyuzu Green Lanes Harringay Turkish grill
w: Opened in 1999 by the Yavuz family, the slickest of Green Lanes' big-four charcoal-grill houses — famous for mixed-grill sharing platters the size of small countries, and queues that build after half past eight. The flagship of Harringay's 'Little Turkey'.

Begun in 1993 as a Green Lanes baklava producer — reputedly London's first authentic baklava maker, from Gaziantep, a UNESCO city of gastronomy. Now a mini-empire of grill, döner and künefe shops, but the trays of pistachio baklava in the window are still the point.

q: Noted Eel and Pie House Leytonstone Hak family
w: Run by the Hak family, descendants of Dutch eel-fishing schuyts — started in Bow in 1926, moved to Leytonstone in the 1970s, and one of the only shops still keeping live eels out the back. It was renamed 'Noted' in the war so the German-looking old sign wouldn't draw suspicion. Now, improbably, a TikTok pilgrimage for the eel-curious.

s: the listed Art Deco pie shop
q: L Manze Walthamstow High Street pie mash Art Deco
w: East London's oldest surviving pie-and-mash building, from 1929, Grade II-listed in 2013 for its complete Art Deco interior — tilework, mirrors, marble. The pie counter itself was reported closed in 2022, but the listing protects one of the most beautiful eel-and-liquor rooms ever built.

Established in 1885 and running the length of the High Street — reputed to be a mile, though it actually measures about a kilometre — with some five hundred stalls. Officially the longest outdoor market in Europe, fully alive six days a week, with L. Manze's Art Deco frontage watching over one end.

q: Bryant and May matchgirls strike Bow Quarter
w: The 1888 matchgirls' strike against phossy-jaw conditions and brutal fines began at this Bryant & May factory — now the gated, gentrified Bow Quarter. Led by women and girls and backed by Annie Besant, it was a hinge moment for the British labour movement. Strike leader Sarah Chapman is commemorated nearby.

q: Minnie Lansbury memorial clock Bow Road
w: A green memorial clock on Bow Road to the suffragette and Poplar councillor Minnie Lansbury, jailed in 1921 for refusing to over-charge her poorest constituents during the Poplar Rates Rebellion. She died soon after her release. The clock keeps her time on the high street she fought for.

s: the Hampton Court of the East
q: Eastbury Manor House Barking Gunpowder Plot
w: A Grade I Elizabethan brick manor of the 1570s, marooned amid a council estate on former Barking Abbey land. Defoe spread the debunked legend that the Gunpowder Plot was hatched here, and resident ghosts are duly reported. A complete Tudor merchant's house, improbably surviving in the far East End.

Dagenham's only surviving manor house, medieval-moated, named for Agnes de Valence. Now a museum with a gloriously eccentric collection — Egyptian ushabti figures, a fragment of Queen Victoria's wedding cake, and the Tsarina Alexandra's walking stick — sitting in green calm where Ford's vast plant once roared nearby.

q: Barking Abbey Curfew Tower St Margaret's Captain Cook
w: The ruins of one of England's most important medieval royal nunneries, founded around 666 AD. The surviving Curfew Tower and St Margaret's Church remain — where Captain Cook married in 1762, before he sailed off the edge of the known map. Lawns and low walls now, but for a thousand years this was real power.

q: Eel Pie Island Twickenham Rolling Stones hotel
w: A tiny Thames island whose vanished hotel hosted the Rolling Stones' five-month 1963 residency — nearly every Wednesday, just before fame — plus the Who, Bowie and the Bluesbreakers. Later it became the UK's largest hippie commune, until a 1971 fire. Now artists' studios, reachable on foot only on twice-yearly open weekends.

q: Eel Pie Island Museum Twickenham Trevor Baylis
w: A tiny museum to the island's rock-and-roll decade and to its inventor-islander Trevor Baylis, who gave the world the wind-up radio. You can sit in his thinking chair. A whole scene's worth of memory packed into a couple of rooms on the Twickenham bank.

s: a modernist box full of skulls
q: Royal College of Physicians Regent's Park Lasdun museum
w: Denys Lasdun's 1964 white modernist building on the edge of Regent's Park — Grade I-listed, all floating planes and a sweeping staircase — hides a museum of medical curiosities, a 'spit-and-polish' collection of anatomical relics, and a medicinal garden of plants that can cure or kill. Free, and far stranger inside than the cool exterior admits.

For years the Italian modernist Italo Svevo — author of 'Confessions of Zeno', friend and English pupil of James Joyce — spent long stretches in Charlton, running the family marine-paint business. A quiet south-east London footnote to one of the strangest careers in European literature: a Trieste novelist commuting to the Thames.

s: a Thai temple behind the tennis
w: The first Thai Buddhist temple built in the UK — a golden-roofed wat set in four acres of Wimbledon garden, with a lake, ornamental trees and an interior of vivid murals painted by Thai artists. A few streets from the tennis, and a complete transplant of another country's sacred architecture into suburban SW19.

A double-roofed Buddhist peace pagoda raised in Battersea Park in 1985 by the monks and nuns of Nipponzan-Myōhōji, its gilded Buddhas facing out across the Thames. A resident monk tended it for decades. A startling note of stillness between the power station and the river.

q: Joseph Grimaldi Park Pentonville musical tomb
w: A former churchyard turned park around the grave of Joseph Grimaldi, the father of modern clowning, who died in 1837. An artist's installation set bronze coffin-shaped tiles into the ground that chime musical notes when you dance across them — so you can, quite literally, play a tune on the clown's tomb.

q: King Henry's Mound Richmond Park St Paul's view
w: A prehistoric mound in Richmond Park where, through a gap in the trees and a fixed telescope, you can see St Paul's Cathedral ten miles away — a sightline protected by law from any building that would block it. Legend says Henry VIII stood here to watch for the rocket signalling Anne Boleyn's execution; the dates don't quite work, but the view does.

q: One Tree Hill Honor Oak Oak of Honor view
w: A wooded south London ridge crowned by the Oak of Honor, where Elizabeth I supposedly picnicked, with a sudden view to the City from the top. The whole area is uniquely encircled by cemeteries — Nunhead, Camberwell Old and New — which has fed a persistent, treat-it-as-folklore local rumour of satanic worship on the hill after dark.

q: The Point Greenwich Hyde Vale viewpoint
w: A small, half-hidden promontory above Greenwich, off the beaten path the tourists take to the Observatory — a sudden grandstand view over the Thames, the towers and the spread of the city. The viewpoint Greenwich locals send you to instead of the queue.

s: 700 wild deer, ten minutes from the Tube
w: Around 600 red and fallow deer roam Richmond Park freely, as they have since Charles I enclosed it for hunting in 1637 — the largest of London's Royal Parks and a National Nature Reserve. In the autumn rut the stags bellow and clash; year-round, keep your distance, especially in birthing season. Genuinely wild, barely zone 4.

The second-largest Royal Park, beside Hampton Court — herds of red and fallow deer grazing among the grasslands and the Chestnut Avenue that Wren laid out as a grand approach. Quieter and wilder than its famous neighbour, with the deer wandering close enough to remind you the place was made for the chase.

Forty-odd hectares of lakes, reedbed and marsh created on four redundant Victorian reservoirs in Barnes — opened in 2000 and now one of the best urban birdwatching sites in Europe, with bitterns, sand martins and wintering wildfowl a short bus ride from Hammersmith. A working argument that cities can manufacture wilderness.

Ten working Thames Water reservoirs, opened to the public in 2017 as Europe's largest urban wetland nature reserve — 211 hectares of water and reedbed on the Lea, with herons, peregrines on the old engine-house chimney, and the city skyline floating beyond. Still supplying London's taps while the birds move in.

Six thousand acres of ancient woodland and heath straddling the city's north-east edge — a former royal hunting forest saved for the public by the Corporation of London in 1878, after locals fought the enclosures. Iron Age earthworks, veteran pollarded hornbeams, and the highwayman Dick Turpin's old country, all within a Central line ride.

A soaring Gothic Congregational chapel of 1877 on Upper Street — still a working church and a homeless drop-in, and one of the most atmospheric music venues in Britain, where the audience sits in the pews under the great rose window. Everyone from Björk to Amy Winehouse has played the room; the acoustics do half the work.

q: Cart and Horses Stratford Iron Maiden
w: A Stratford pub that bills itself the birthplace of Iron Maiden — Steve Harris's band cut their teeth on its tiny stage in the mid-1970s, and the heavy-metal pilgrimage has never really stopped. Loud, unpretentious, and proud of a noise that went on to fill stadiums.

Britain's leading dance house, on a Clerkenwell site that has held a theatre since 1683 — named for Richard Sadler and the medieval holy well found in his grounds. The present building is the sixth on the spot; the well itself still survives under the stalls, beneath the world's contemporary choreography.

A weekend food market strung along the Ropewalk under the Bermondsey railway arches — born when traders spilled out of Borough, scrappier and better for it. St John doughnuts, gin from a working distillery, cheese toasties and natural wine, eaten standing as the trains rumble overhead.

A street market running since at least the seventeenth century, now the weekday lunch engine of Clerkenwell — a long line of stalls slinging everything from Ethiopian wraps to grilled cheese, with the white-collar queue stretching down the lane by half past twelve. Trade, not heritage, but five hundred years of it.

s: a food hall in a Pugin church
q: Mercato Mayfair St Mark's North Audley Street
w: A sustainable food hall built inside St Mark's, a deconsecrated Grade I Mayfair church partly by Pugin's circle — traders and a wine bar arranged beneath the nave, the organ pipes and stained glass still overhead. Communion replaced by carbonara, in one of the more surreal reuses of sacred space in London.

s: lunch in the old bike shed
w: Hidden behind a blue door in the wall of a former Victorian school off Arnold Circus, in what was the bicycle shed — Margot Henderson's spare, brilliant British canteen, with a short daily-changing menu and tables in the old playground. You have to know it's there, which is rather the point.

s: the pub that vanishes under flowers
w: A Kensington corner pub buried under a cascade of flowers — hundreds of baskets and a few hundred pounds a month in blooms — with a Christmas display of nearly a hundred trees. Inside, Churchilliana, butterflies and bric-a-brac, and one of London's first pub Thai kitchens, going since the late 1980s in the conservatory out back.

s: wine and cheese in the gents
q: WC Clapham wine bar Victorian toilets
w: A wine-and-charcuterie bar built into a disused Victorian underground public lavatory on Clapham Common — hence the name, 'Wine & Charcuterie', and the joke. Original tiling, cisterns repurposed, and a list of low-intervention bottles served below pavement level, where Londoners once did something altogether less convivial.

s: the pink room and the egg pods
w: A Mayfair townhouse turned surrealist playground — the blush-pink Gallery hung with David Shrigley's drawings, and a set of pod-shaped, egg-like toilet capsules under a stained-glass dome that have launched a million photos. Tea, art and theatre, deliberately too much of everything. Go for the rooms as much as the food.

s: fifty years of the movement, shelved
w: A community-run archive founded in 1975, holding tens of thousands of books, periodicals and the paper records of the Women's Liberation Movement — pamphlets, badges, banners, the ephemera that institutions threw away. After years of precarious moves it found a Peckham home; a living memory of a revolution, kept by volunteers.

q: Conway Hall Red Lion Square humanism library
w: Opened in 1929 and run by the Conway Hall Ethical Society, the oldest free-thought organisation in the world — a Holborn hall that has hosted radicals, humanists and protest since before the war, with a Humanist Library and a Sunday concert series stretching back over a century. Reason and dissent, with good acoustics.

q: Museum of London Docklands West India Quay
w: Inside a Grade I Georgian sugar warehouse on West India Quay — a free museum of the river, the port and the trade that built London, including 'London, Sugar & Slavery', a permanent gallery confronting the city's role in the transatlantic slave economy. The building itself is the exhibit: brick, beams, and a very uncomfortable history.

q: Bethnal Green Working Men's Club cabaret
w: A genuine 1953 working men's club that became one of East London's great cabaret and club nights — flock wallpaper, a sprung dance floor, glitter and drag and swing under the old members' photos. The committee-room formality and the chaos of the night sit on top of each other, and somehow it works.

q: Ragged School Museum Mile End Copperfield Road
w: Canalside warehouses that once held Dr Barnardo's largest 'ragged school', educating the poorest East End children for free between 1877 and 1908. The museum recreates a Victorian classroom — slates, cane, hard benches — and a 1900 East End kitchen, in a building that was the closest thing thousands of children got to a chance.

A rare surviving Georgian townhouse in a hidden City square, where Samuel Johnson compiled his great Dictionary of the English Language in the attic between 1748 and 1755, with six clerks at long tables. The garret survives, the books survive, and outside sits Hodge, his cat, in bronze — 'a very fine cat indeed'.

Built in the 1890s as the estate office of William Waldorf Astor, then the richest man in the world — a small neo-Gothic mansion stuffed with carved oak, stained glass, silver-gilt and characters from Dumas and Arthurian legend, money turned into fantasy. Free to enter during its winter exhibitions, and almost unknown.

q: Middle Temple Hall Elizabethan Twelfth Night
w: A magnificent Elizabethan hall of 1573 in the lawyers' warren of the Inns of Court, under a double hammer-beam roof, with a serving table said to be made from the timbers of Drake's Golden Hinde. Shakespeare's 'Twelfth Night' had what may be its premiere here in 1602 — performed for the barristers in the very room that still stands.

s: the gig pub that fought the wreckers
w: A battered, beautiful old coaching inn on Commercial Road that became a fierce little music and arts venue — and then spent years fighting off developers whose flats next door would have killed its noise. Shot by photographers and bands for its peeling grandeur; saved, for now, by the people who love it.

s: a Sephardic field inside a university
q: Novo Cemetery Queen Mary Mile End Sephardic
w: A Sephardic Jewish burial ground from 1733, the resting place of London's Spanish and Portuguese community — now improbably surrounded by the campus of Queen Mary University, students crossing between worn flat ledger-stones laid in the old Iberian style. A piece of the seventeenth-century diaspora preserved among the lecture halls.

s: press your ear to a lost river
q: Fleet River Ray Street grating Clerkenwell
w: London's largest buried river, the Fleet, runs in a sewer beneath the streets — and on Ray Street in Clerkenwell there is a grating in the road where, if you stop and listen over the traffic, you can actually hear it rushing below. A whole river, demoted to a drain, still audible if you know where to stand.

s: two ears on a Covent Garden wall
q: Floral Street ears sculpture Tim Fishlock
w: High on a wall in a Covent Garden alley, two small sculpted ears — cast by the artist Tim Fishlock and left to eavesdrop on the passing street. Most people walk under them for years without ever looking up. The building has been listening the whole time.

s: feral parrots in the royal park
w: Flocks of bright green ring-necked parakeets — escaped or released, and now thoroughly Londoner — swirl through Kensington Gardens and Hyde Park, where they'll land on an outstretched hand for seed near the Italian Gardens. A tropical absurdity gone fully native, screeching through the planes above the joggers.

s: an open-air pool in the West End
q: Oasis Sports Centre heated outdoor pool Holborn
w: A heated outdoor swimming pool in the middle of Holborn, hiding behind Covent Garden — you swim lengths under the open sky with office towers leaning in on every side. Improbable, slightly surreal, and beloved by Londoners who like to do their morning crawl with the city watching.

London's most storied nightclub, open since 1999 in a former Smithfield cold store — home of Room One's 'bodysonic' floor, wired so the bass comes up through your feet. It was briefly shut down in 2016 and saved by a global outcry; the all-night crowd files in past the meat market still.

An old Memorial Working Men's Club in Hackney, its ceiling drowned in gold tinsel, that doubles as one of East London's best small gig and club rooms — sweaty, sticky-floored and gloriously incongruous, with veterans' photos on the walls and a guitar band roaring under the glitter.

s: the Saturday market by the canal
w: A market street between London Fields and the Regent's Canal, trading since the nineteenth century and reborn as Hackney's Saturday ritual — cheese, oysters, flat whites and flowers, the towpath busy with bikes. Gentrified, certainly, but built on a genuinely old line of trade from the fields to the city.

s: the home of Black British history
q: Black Cultural Archives Windrush Square Brixton
w: The only national archive dedicated to the history of African and Caribbean people in Britain — founded in 1981 out of the Brixton uprisings, and since 2014 housed in a restored Georgian building on Windrush Square. Records, photographs and papers that no mainstream institution was keeping; a community insisting on its own memory.

A free cultural institute and library opened in 1895 beside Liverpool Street — home to extraordinary collections the establishment overlooked: protest, the co-operative movement, LGBTQ+ history, London's own ephemera. An ornate Arts and Crafts reading room, an open door, and a quiet insistence that ordinary people's records matter.

s: Behind an ordinary bungalow, a staircase drops 125ft into a three-storey cold-war bunker built to run Britain after a nuclear strike
A medieval hamlet swallowed by Metro-land that still holds a royally chartered street fair, granted in 1336
A stepped memorial on the field where cavalry charged a peaceful 60,000-strong reform rally in 1819, killing eighteen
q: Peterloo Massacre memorial St Peter's Field Manchester

s: A mill district that became Manchester's Little Italy, whose vendors gave Britain the ice-cream cone — patented locally in 1901
q: Ancoats Little Italy Manchester ice cream cone

s: A 1903 youth club immortalised on the inner sleeve of The Smiths' The Queen Is Dead
access: Working youth club — limited hours; photo spot outside free

s: The Factory Records club that birthed Madchester and acid house, lost money on nearly every clubber, then was demolished for flats that took its name
Where a machine of war-surplus parts ran the world's first stored program in 1948 — the literal birth of software
q: Manchester Baby Small-Scale Experimental Machine plaque
access: Plaque on campus; working replica at the Science and Industry Museum (free)

s: 112ft beneath Chinatown, a nuclear-hardened cold-war telephone bunker with its own bar, piano and fake painted windows
q: Guardian Underground Telephone Exchange Manchester GUTE
access: No public access — entrances are anonymous doorways

s: The former warehouse where 1844 weavers opened a shop on a plank-and-barrel counter, launching the global co-operative movement
access: Free entry; check current opening days

s: A 1906 water palace of stained glass and mosaic, saved from dereliction when it won the BBC's first Restoration series
access: Open on certain days/tours (seasonal) — check schedule

s: The English-speaking world's oldest free public library (1653), in a 1421 complex, where Marx and Engels read together in a window alcove
access: Readers by appointment Mon–Fri; guided tours bookable

s: A market boozer named for a Depression-era landlord who built the stage halfway up the back wall to cram more drinkers below
q: Band on the Wall Swan Street Manchester
access: Active venue; ticketed gigs and bar hours

s: A green-tiled Victorian island marooned among glass towers
q: Peveril of the Peak pub Manchester

s: An arrow on the quayside marks a 215-year-old trapdoor draining the canal into a buried Roman-era river
q: River Tib trapdoor Lock 89 Rochdale Canal Manchester

s: A vaulted underground wine bar with a cult jukebox, hidden off St Ann's Square
q: Corbieres Wine Cavern Half Moon Street Manchester

s: The world's first electric elevated railway ran 60 years above the docks as the Dockers' Umbrella, then was scrapped so completely barely a column survives
access: Carriage at Museum of Liverpool (free); remnants at street level

s: A reclusive tobacco merchant had gangs dig a labyrinth of grand brick tunnels under Edge Hill for no recorded reason
The real birthplace of the Beatles' live career was a suburban coal cellar, its ceilings still hand-painted by the band
q: Casbah Coffee Club West Derby Liverpool

s: The only purpose-built Victorian pub listed Grade I — its rose-marble gents' toilets are a sightseeing attraction
q: Philharmonic Dining Rooms Hope Street Liverpool

s: Behind a plain brick exterior hides the finest Moorish-Revival synagogue in Britain
access: Active synagogue — pre-booked guided tour only

s: A sunken cemetery carved from a quarry below the cathedral, with rock-cut catacombs and the domed tomb of the first man killed by a train
q: St James's Cemetery Gardens Liverpool Huskisson

s: Four near-derelict terraces saved by residents who painted and planted them through decades of managed decline — winning Assemble the 2015 Turner Prize
q: Granby Four Streets Toxteth Assemble Turner Prize
access: Public streets; monthly market; Winter Garden by arrangement

s: A roofless Gothic shell kept exactly as the May 1941 Blitz left it — Liverpool's last surviving bomb site
q: St Luke's Bombed Out Church Liverpool
access: Grounds seasonal; exterior free any time

s: The last freestanding pub of the old dock road
A domed Victorian glasshouse in Liverpool's grandest park
A dance hall built on stilts above a barrow-market, crowned by one of the UK's first animated neon signs — and shadowed by the unsolved Bible John murders
access: Ticketed gigs; Barras market weekends; sign visible any time

s: Mackintosh's last major Glasgow commission — a 1906 school with soaring leaded-glass towers — closed with just 89 pupils as shipbuilding collapsed
q: Scotland Street School Museum Mackintosh Glasgow

s: In a church ringed by former shipyards sit 31 early-medieval stones — including five giant Viking-age hogbacks — relics of the lost kingdom of Strathclyde
access: Open daily 1 Apr–31 Oct, free; tours by arrangement

n: Duke of Wellington Statue & Traffic Cone
s: An 1844 equestrian statue Glaswegians have insisted on crowning with a traffic cone since the 1980s — a 2013 council plan to stop it collapsed within a day
q: Duke of Wellington statue traffic cone Glasgow

s: The stream where St Mungo is said to have founded Glasgow now runs hidden in a culvert beneath Wishart Street
access: Wishart St & Bridge of Sighs publicly accessible

s: An Edwardian flat frozen in time by a typist who threw nothing away for 54 years — including a jar of plum jam made in 1929, beneath still-working gas lights
A Victorian city of the dead of 3,500 monuments on a hill above the cathedral, built multi-faith — its first burial a Jewish jeweller
access: Free, open access; tours by the Friends

s: The world's oldest surviving music hall — where a 16-year-old Stan Laurel debuted in 1906 — hidden above an arcade behind a false ceiling until 1997
access: Viewing certain days (Thu–Sat) & events — check; no heating

s: Behind an inconspicuous close off Argyle Street, a maze of cobbled, rainbow-painted yards with 100-plus artists' studios
access: Lane open; studios keep own hours (best Tue–Sat)

s: Glasgow's only country park — a Highland cattle fold and an Edwardian house full of Spanish art
A live-music venue and club inside a moored 1950s cargo ship
An 18th-century castle folly and a hamlet of Nash cottages on the city's edge
q: Blaise Castle Estate Hamlet Henbury Bristol
access: Estate free, open access; museum seasonal

s: 850 acres of deer park and ancient oaks around a half-derelict mansion
The empty plinth where the slave-trader's statue stood until protesters threw it in the harbour in 2020
access: Public; plinth viewable any time (statue now in M Shed)

s: The most-walked necropolis in the world — but it's the mossy cobbled lanes, not the famous graves, that earn it
